transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hi Zac, I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Uh, thanks. I guess... I don’t know, there’s just been a lot on my mind lately. You know, things at work haven’t been going great. I tried to suggest something new for teaching, but it didn’t go well. Just feels like I keep hitting walls.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’ve been feeling frustrated with your work, especially after trying to introduce something new in your teaching. It must be tough to feel like you’re hitting walls. Can you tell me more about what happened when you made that suggestion?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, I thought it was a good idea, something that could really help the students. But when I brought it up, the leadership just... rejected it outright. No real discussion or anything. It felt like they didn’t even consider it. I guess I just felt powerless in that moment, like my opinion didn’t matter at all. It’s hard to shake off that feeling.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you put a lot of thought into your suggestion, and it was disappointing to have it dismissed without discussion. Feeling powerless in that moment must have been really challenging, especially when you care about helping your students. How has that experience affected you since then?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s been... frustrating. I’ve found myself just going through the motions at work. I don’t really want to engage much anymore. I keep thinking about how unreasonable the decision was, but I don’t say anything to anyone. It’s like, what’s the point? I just feel kind of stuck, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It seems like you’re feeling stuck and frustrated, which is understandable given what you experienced. Going through the motions can feel draining, especially when you’re holding onto those feelings about the decision. It sounds like there’s a part of you that wants to express how you feel but isn’t sure if it would make a difference. What do you think is holding you back from sharing your thoughts with others?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess I just don’t feel like I have the authority to speak up. It’s like, who am I to challenge the leadership? I hate feeling powerless, but it’s hard to shake that off. I worry that if I say something, it’ll just make things worse or that I’ll look foolish. So, I keep it to myself and just... deal with it, I suppose.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re grappling with a lot of self-doubt and concern about how speaking up might be perceived. Feeling like you lack the authority to challenge leadership can be really limiting, especially when you have valuable ideas. It’s understandable to want to avoid making things worse or feeling foolish. How do you typically cope with those feelings of powerlessness when they come up?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I don’t know, I guess I just... try to distract myself. I talk about it with a few friends, but it’s mostly just venting. I don’t really know if that helps, though. Sometimes I feel like I’m just being defiant in my own way, but it doesn’t really change anything. It’s like I’m stuck in this loop of frustration and not knowing what to do next.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like venting to friends provides a bit of an outlet for your frustration, but it doesn’t feel like it leads to any real change. That loop of feeling frustrated and unsure of what to do next can be really exhausting. It’s understandable to want to find a way out of that cycle. What do you think would help you feel more empowered or able to take action in this situation?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’m not sure, honestly. Part of me thinks maybe I need to find a way to build my confidence, but I don’t know how to do that. I guess if I felt more supported or if I had someone backing me up, it might help. It’s just hard to see a way forward when I feel like my ideas aren’t valued. I wish I could find a way to express myself without feeling so vulnerable.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It makes sense that feeling supported and valued could help you build your confidence. It’s tough to express yourself when you feel vulnerable, especially after experiencing rejection. It sounds like you’re looking for a way to share your ideas in a way that feels safer. What do you think would make it easier for you to express yourself without feeling so exposed?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe if I had a clearer plan or some kind of evidence to back up my ideas, it would help. Like, if I could show how my method could actually benefit the students, maybe that would make it easier to present. I think having some support from colleagues would help too, like if I wasn’t alone in it. But even thinking about it makes me feel a bit anxious. It’s just hard to put myself out there again after feeling like I failed.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like having a clear plan and evidence to support your ideas could give you more confidence in presenting them. And having colleagues on your side would definitely make it feel less daunting. It’s completely normal to feel anxious about putting yourself out there again after a setback. That fear of failure can be really heavy. What do you think might help you manage that anxiety as you consider taking those steps?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess I could try to break it down into smaller steps, maybe start by discussing my ideas informally with a trusted colleague first. That way, it wouldn’t feel so overwhelming. I could also remind myself that it’s okay to fail sometimes; it doesn’t mean I’m not capable. But, honestly, it’s still hard to shake off that feeling of doubt. I just don’t want to feel humiliated again.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Breaking it down into smaller steps sounds like a practical approach, and discussing your ideas with a trusted colleague could provide some support and validation. It’s great that you’re recognizing that failure is part of the process and doesn’t define your capabilities. That lingering doubt and fear of humiliation can be tough to navigate. What do you think might help you challenge those doubts when they come up?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe I could try to remind myself of past successes, even if they feel small. It might help to focus on what I’ve done well instead of just the setbacks. I could also try to reframe my thoughts when I start feeling that doubt creeping in, like telling myself that it’s okay to take risks. But, honestly, it’s easier said than done. I still worry about how others will perceive me.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you have some good strategies in mind, like reminding yourself of past successes and reframing your thoughts. It’s completely understandable to worry about how others perceive you, especially when you’re feeling vulnerable. It can be a challenge to shift that focus from doubt to self-encouragement. What do you think might help you practice those strategies in a way that feels more manageable?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe I could start by journaling or something, just to get my thoughts out and reflect on my successes. It might help me see things more clearly. I could also set aside some time to practice those reframing techniques when I’m feeling calm, so they’re more accessible when I’m anxious. But I still think it’ll take time to really believe in myself again. It’s a process, I guess.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Journaling sounds like a wonderful way to reflect on your thoughts and successes, and practicing those reframing techniques during calmer moments could make them easier to access when you need them. It’s great that you recognize this as a process; building self-belief often takes time and patience. How do you feel about giving yourself that time and space to grow through this?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s important, but I also struggle with being patient with myself. I want to see progress quickly, and when I don’t, it’s easy to get frustrated. I know that growth takes time, but I guess I just have to remind myself that it’s okay to take things one step at a time. It’s just hard to let go of that pressure sometimes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely understandable to want to see quick progress, especially when you’re feeling frustrated. The pressure to move forward can be overwhelming, and it’s tough to balance that with the understanding that growth is often gradual. It’s good that you’re recognizing the importance of taking things one step at a time. What do you think might help you manage that pressure and cultivate more patience with yourself as you navigate this process?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe setting smaller, more achievable goals could help. That way, I can celebrate those little wins along the way instead of just focusing on the bigger picture. I could also try to remind myself that everyone has their own pace, and it’s okay if mine is different. Practicing self-compassion might be something to work on too, like treating myself with the same kindness I’d offer a friend. It’s just... sometimes hard to remember to do that for myself.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Setting smaller, achievable goals sounds like a great way to create a sense of progress and celebrate those wins. It’s also really insightful to recognize that everyone has their own pace, and practicing self-compassion can be a powerful tool. It can be challenging to extend that kindness to ourselves, especially when we’re feeling pressure. What do you think might help you remember to practice self-compassion when those moments of doubt arise?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe I could create some reminders for myself, like notes or quotes that encourage self-compassion. Having something tangible to look at might help when I’m feeling down. I could also try to incorporate moments of mindfulness or reflection into my routine, just to check in with myself and acknowledge my feelings without judgment. It’s just... I worry that I’ll forget to do it when I need it most.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Creating reminders and incorporating mindfulness into your routine sounds like a thoughtful approach to fostering self-compassion. It’s natural to worry about forgetting those practices when you need them most, especially during challenging moments. What do you think could help you integrate those reminders and mindfulness practices into your daily life so they become more automatic?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe I could set a specific time each day to check in with myself, like in the morning or before bed. If I make it a part of my routine, it might become more automatic. I could also keep those reminders in places I see often, like on my desk or phone. It’s just about finding a way to make it feel less like a chore and more like something I genuinely want to do. I guess it’s about building that habit over time.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a solid plan! Setting a specific time each day for self-check-ins can help make it a natural part of your routine. Placing reminders in visible spots can also serve as gentle nudges to practice self-compassion. It’s great that you’re thinking about how to make this feel more enjoyable rather than like a chore. Building habits takes time, and it’s okay to be gentle with yourself in that process. How do you feel about starting this new routine? What excites you about it?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit hopeful about it, honestly. The idea of having a routine that focuses on self-compassion feels like it could be a positive shift. I think it might help me feel more grounded and less overwhelmed by those negative thoughts. It’s exciting to think that I could start to change how I view myself, even just a little bit. I guess I’m just a bit nervous about sticking to it, but I’m willing to give it a try.
